name: Machine Lat Pulldown
instructions: Adjust the thigh pad so your knees are firmly secured. Grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lean back slightly, pull the bar down to your upper chest, and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Slowly return the bar to the starting position with control. Exhale on the pull, inhale on the return.
feelRight: You should feel your latissimus dorsi engaging as you pull the bar down. The movement should feel smooth and controlled without jerking.
feelWrong: Using momentum or leaning too far back reduces lat engagement. Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck, which stresses the shoulder joints.
notes: Focus on pulling with your elbows rather than your hands. Control the eccentric phase for at least two seconds to maximize muscle growth.

name: Seated Machine Row
instructions: Sit on the machine with your chest against the pad and feet flat on the platform. Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral grip. Pull the handles toward your torso, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the peak. Extend your arms fully on the return without rounding your shoulders forward.
feelRight: You should feel the contraction in your mid-back and rhomboids as you pull back. The movement should originate from your shoulders, not your lower back.
feelWrong: Rounding the lower back or using excessive body momentum shifts tension away from the target muscles. Avoid pulling with just your arms — lead with your elbows.
notes: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the pad throughout. A 2-second hold at the peak contraction enhances back development.

name: Machine Chest Press
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles align with mid-chest level. Grip the handles and press forward until your arms are fully extended without locking your elbows. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly return the handles to the starting position. Keep your shoulders pinned back against the pad throughout.
feelRight: You should feel the push coming from your pectorals and triceps. The motion should be smooth and symmetrical on both sides.
feelWrong: Flaring your elbows out excessively shifts load to the shoulders. Avoid bouncing the weight off the stack at the end range of motion.
notes: Maintain a neutral wrist position throughout the press. Exhale forcefully during the concentric phase for better power output.

name: Machine Leg Press
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine and place your feet shoulder-width apart on the platform. Release the safety handles and lower the platform by bending your knees to a 90-degree angle. Press through your heels to extend your legs without locking your knees. Control the descent and avoid letting the weight stack slam down.
feelRight: You should feel the work distributed across your quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes. The movement should feel powerful and stable through your entire foot.
feelWrong: Letting your knees cave inward during the press places stress on the ACL. Lifting your heels off the platform shifts load to your toes and reduces glute activation.
notes: Do not lock your knees at full extension to maintain constant tension. Adjust foot placement higher on the platform to emphasize glutes and hamstrings.

name: Machine Shoulder Press
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at shoulder height. Grasp the handles and press upward until your arms are extended overhead without locking out. Lower the handles slowly back to shoulder height. Keep your core braced and your back flat against the pad.
feelRight: You should feel the deltoids doing the primary work, especially the front and side heads. The path should feel natural and arcing slightly forward.
feelWrong: Arching your lower back excessively to push heavier weight shifts risk to the lumbar spine. Avoid shrugging your shoulders up during the press.
notes: Keep your elbows slightly in front of the bar path rather than flared out to the sides. Inhale at the bottom and exhale as you press up.

name: Machine Leg Curl
instructions: Lie face down on the leg curl machine with your knees just off the edge of the pad and the roller pad placed against the back of your ankles. Curl your heels toward your glutes in a smooth, controlled motion. Squeeze your hamstrings at the peak contraction, then lower slowly. Keep your hips pressed into the pad throughout.
feelRight: You should feel a deep contraction in your hamstrings as you curl the weight up. The movement should feel isolated with no lower back strain.
feelWrong: Jerking the weight up or using momentum reduces hamstring activation and can strain the lower back. Avoid lifting your hips off the pad to gain leverage.
notes: Pointing your toes slightly during the curl can increase hamstring engagement. Lower the weight slowly over 3 seconds for maximal tension.

name: Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a straight bar attached to the high pulley. Grip the bar with hands shoulder-width apart, palms facing down. Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and push the bar down until your arms are fully extended. Pause at the bottom, then slowly return the bar to chest height.
feelRight: You should feel the burn concentrated in the triceps, especially the lateral head. The movement should come purely from elbow extension with no shoulder involvement.
feelWrong: Letting your elbows drift forward or out to the sides turns the exercise into a pressing movement. Using your upper body to swing the weight down reduces tricep isolation.
notes: Lean forward slightly and keep a slight bend in your knees for stability. Squeeze the triceps hard at full extension for one full second.

name: Machine Leg Extension
instructions: Sit on the leg extension machine with your back against the pad and the roller bar resting across the top of your shins. Extend your legs until they are straight without locking your knees. Squeeze your quadriceps at the top, then lower the weight with control. Do not let the weight drop abruptly.
feelRight: You should feel a strong contraction across your entire quadriceps, especially the teardrop (vastus medialis). The motion should feel isolated to the knee joint.
feelWrong: Snapping your knees into full lockout transfers stress to the joint rather than the muscle. Kicking the weight up with momentum reduces eccentric tension.
notes: Point your toes slightly outward to emphasize the outer quad sweep. Lower the weight over a 3-second count for hypertrophic benefit.

name: Machine Seated Calf Raise
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine with the pads resting on your thighs just above your knees. Place the balls of your feet on the foot platform with your heels hanging off. Raise your heels as high as possible by pressing through the balls of your feet. Lower your heels slowly below the platform to achieve a full stretch at the bottom.
feelRight: You should feel a deep stretch in your soleus at the bottom and a strong contraction at the top. The movement should be slow and deliberate.
feelWrong: Bouncing at the bottom uses tendon elasticity instead of muscle work. Raising your heels too quickly skips the stretch reflex in the calves.
notes: Keep the reps slow with a 2-second hold at the top. Seated calf raises target the soleus more than standing variations.

name: Machine Pectoral Fly
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles align with chest height. Grasp the handles and bring your arms together in front of your chest in a wide arc. Squeeze your pecs together at the front of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your shoulders depressed and back against the pad.
feelRight: You should feel a deep stretch across your pectorals at the open position and a strong contraction at the peak. The arc should feel natural and controlled.
feelWrong: Using too much weight causes you to press instead of fly, reducing chest isolation. Letting your shoulders roll forward at the stretch point increases injury risk.
notes: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout — do not lock them out. Focus on bringing your elbows together, not your hands.

name: Machine Hip Adductor
instructions: Sit on the adductor machine with your back against the pad and your legs placed outside the movement arms. Adjust the range of motion so you feel a gentle stretch at the starting position. Squeeze your legs together by contracting your inner thighs, then slowly release. Control the weight on both the concentric and eccentric phases.
feelRight: You should feel the contraction deep in your adductors (inner thighs) with no pinching in the hips. The movement should feel smooth throughout the full range.
feelWrong: Using momentum to swing the weight together reduces adductor engagement. Avoid cranking the range of motion too wide, which can strain the groin.
notes: Keep your feet stationary on the footrests to maintain proper alignment. Exhale during the adduction phase for better core stability.

name: Machine Hip Abductor
instructions: Sit on the abductor machine with your back flat against the pad and your legs inside the movement arms. Press your legs outward against the pads by engaging your glutes and outer hips. Pause briefly at the peak contraction, then slowly return to the starting position. Avoid leaning forward or using your torso to assist.
feelRight: You should feel the activation in your gluteus medius and minimus as you open your legs. The movement should feel controlled and isolated to the hips.
feelWrong: Leaning forward or gripping the handles too tightly recruits the core and upper body instead of the target glute muscles. Letting the weight slam back reduces eccentric benefit.
notes: Keep your toes pointing forward or slightly inward to maximize glute medius activation. Perform these slowly with a focus on the mind-muscle connection.

name: Cable Woodchopper
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position with a standard handle attachment. Stand sideways to the machine, feet shoulder-width apart. Grasp the handle with both hands over one shoulder. Pull the handle diagonally down and across your body toward the opposite hip, rotating your torso and pivoting your back foot slightly. Control the weight as you slowly return to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel strong activation in your obliques, core, and shoulders. The movement should feel controlled and deliberate, driven primarily by torso rotation rather than just arm movement.
feelWrong: Pulling the weight using only your arms while keeping your torso stationary defeats the purpose of the exercise. Twisting your lower back excessively without pivoting your hips can lead to strain.
notes: Keep your arms relatively straight with a slight bend in the elbows throughout the motion. You can also perform this exercise from low-to-high or horizontally to target the core from different angles.

name: Cable Pull-Through
instructions: Attach a rope to a low pulley on a cable machine. Stand facing away from the machine, straddling the cable. Reach between your legs and grasp the rope with both hands. Walk forward a few steps to create tension. Keeping a flat back and a slight bend in your knees, hinge forward at the hips, letting the cable pull your hands between your legs. Drive your hips forward, squeezing your glutes to stand back up to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom and a hard contraction in your glutes at the top. The motion is a pure hip hinge.
feelWrong: Squatting down instead of hinging back shifts the focus to the quads. Rounding the lower back puts dangerous stress on the lumbar spine.
notes: Keep your arms straight and relatively relaxed; they are just hooks holding the rope. The power comes entirely from the hips.
